beats.headline: A missed patch, unseen for weeks.
beats.body: A known flaw in a public web app went unpatched. With monitoring crippled by an expired security certificate, the intrusion ran undetected for about 76 days while data on roughly 147 million people was taken.
say: Every log, every record, every trace — I will erase it.
Not tonight. Even if you slip in, you will not move unseen.

beats.title: INSIDE THE WALLS
say: I slipped a careless credential past your wall. Now I move in the shadows.
Shadows don't last here. The watchtowers never sleep.

beats.title: THE WATCHTOWERS
beats.subtitle: Audit · Integrity · Incident Response
say: Layer two assumes someone got in. Now we see them, stop the rot, and respond.
Three families keep watch: Audit, System Integrity, and Incident Response.

beats.title: THE WATCHTOWERS
say: AU, SI, and IR. The eyes, the immune system, and the firefighters.

beats.protects: Records every footstep, so no deed goes unseen.
beats.reality: Logging security-relevant events and being able to review them and hold actors accountable.
say: It records every footstep, so no deed goes unseen.
In reality, that's logging security-relevant events so you can review them and hold people accountable.

beats.plain: A timestamped record of what happened on a system — who signed in, what they touched, what changed. It is the evidence you read after something goes wrong.
beats.example: Like a building's sign-in sheet and camera timeline, but for a computer system.
say: A diary the system keeps. A Windows Event Log, a syslog, a tool like Splunk — same idea.

beats.title: Event Logging
beats.plain: Decide which events the system logs to support the audit function.
beats.why: You can't review what you never recorded; logging is where accountability begins.
say: So I'll just delete the logs when I'm done.

beats.title: Protection of Audit Information
beats.plain: Protect audit information and logging tools from unauthorized change or deletion.
beats.why: This is what stops an intruder from erasing their own tracks.
say: Not so fast. AU-9 protects the logs themselves.
So the Null can't quietly rewrite history.

beats.quote: Identify the types of events that the system is capable of logging in support of the audit function:
say: Identify the types of events that the system is capable of logging in support of the audit function:

beats.title: Audit and Accountability
bullets: AU-2 Event Logging — decide what events to log
AU-3 Content of Audit Records — capture enough detail in each record
AU-6 Audit Record Review, Analysis, and Reporting — review and analyze the logs
AU-9 Protection of Audit Information — protect the logs from tampering
say: A log no one reads, or one that can be erased, is no log at all.

beats.oath: Every footstep written; no page erased.
say: The Chronicler's oath. Every footstep written; no page erased.

beats.protects: Finds the rot, cures the sickness, keeps the truth true.
beats.reality: Flaw remediation, malicious-code protection, system monitoring, and integrity verification.
say: The Healer, System and Information Integrity.
It finds the rot, cures the sickness, and keeps the truth true.
In reality, that's patching flaws, blocking malicious code, monitoring for intrusions, and verifying nothing was tampered with.

beats.title: Flaw Remediation
beats.plain: Find, report, and fix system flaws — and patch them in good time.
beats.why: Unpatched flaws are the cracks attackers pour through; SI-2 seals them.
say: SI-2 is flaw remediation, the discipline of patching.

beats.title: System Monitoring
beats.plain: Monitor the system to detect attacks, intrusions, and unusual activity.
beats.why: It's the tower's spyglass, watching traffic and behavior for trouble.
say: And SI-4 watches the system for signs of an intruder.
Another tower turns? Tedious. Every chain of watchers has a blind link — and I will slip through it.

beats.title: THE WATCH CATCHES A SHADOW
say: Six days I moved in your shadows before a single tower turned.
Six days — but SI-4 saw the anomaly, and AU-9 kept the logs he tripped. He got in. He did not get out clean.

beats.quote: Identify, report, and correct system flaws;
say: Identify, report, and correct system flaws;

beats.title: System and Information Integrity
bullets: SI-2 Flaw Remediation — patch flaws promptly
SI-3 Malicious Code Protection — stop malicious code
SI-4 System Monitoring — monitor for intrusions
SI-7 Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity — verify software and data integrity
say: Integrity is keeping the system clean and honest.

beats.oath: Find the rot, close the wound, keep it true.
say: The Healer's oath. Find the rot, close the wound, keep it true.

beats.protects: Sounds the alarm and contains the blaze.
beats.reality: Detecting, reporting, analyzing, containing, and recovering from security incidents.
say: When something burns, it sounds the alarm and contains the blaze.
In reality, that's detecting, reporting, analyzing, containing, and recovering from security incidents.

beats.plain: An event that actually harms a system or its data, or breaks the rules meant to protect them — a real breach, not just a warning light.
beats.example: A ransomware infection, a stolen laptop full of patient data, or a hijacked account.
say: When does a threat become an 'incident'?
The moment it stops being hypothetical — malware runs, data leaks, a system goes down.

beats.title: Incident Handling
beats.plain: Run a capability to detect, contain, eradicate, and recover from incidents.
beats.why: A breach is a question of when; IR-4 is having the answer rehearsed.
say: What happens the moment we spot the Null?
IR-4 takes over, handling the incident end to end.

beats.title: The incident response lifecycle
say: Every incident follows a rhythm: prepare, detect, contain, eradicate, recover.
And IR-8 writes the plan that makes it routine.

beats.quote: Implement an incident handling capability for incidents that is consistent with the incident response plan and includes preparation, detection and analysis, containment, eradication, and recovery;
say: Implement an incident handling capability for incidents that is consistent with the incident response plan and includes preparation, detection and analysis, containment, eradication, and recovery;

beats.title: Incident Response
bullets: IR-2 Incident Response Training — train people for their incident roles
IR-4 Incident Handling — handle incidents end to end
IR-6 Incident Reporting — report incidents to the right people
IR-8 Incident Response Plan — write and maintain the response plan
say: The best responders practiced before the fire.

beats.oath: When it burns, we are already running.
say: The Firewatch's oath. When it burns, we are already running.

beats.title: The Watchtowers in the RMF
bullets: AU, SI, and IR power the MONITOR step — continuous vigilance
They embody the Detect and Respond security functions
Protect your logs (AU-9) or the whole watch can be blinded
say: Selecting and implementing was the wall. Watching is how you keep it.

beats.q: An intruder tries to delete the logs to hide. Which control blocks that?
say: Scenario: the Null deletes logs. What stops it? Pause, and answer.
beats.why: AU-9, Protection of Audit Information, keeps an intruder from deleting or altering the logs.

beats.q: Which family detects, contains, and recovers from a breach?
say: And who runs toward the fire?
beats.why: Incident Response is the family that detects, contains, and recovers from a breach.

beats.title: Episode 02 — the Watchtowers
lines: AU sees and remembers — and protects its own records (AU-2, AU-6, AU-9).
SI keeps the system clean and honest: patch, detect, verify (SI-2, SI-4, SI-7).
IR turns a crisis into a rehearsed routine (IR-4, IR-6, IR-8).
beats.mnemonic: Log it, protect it, read it — then run toward the fire.
say: Notebook: assume they got in, then see them, heal the system, and answer fast.
Seen, healed, and answered. The towers hold.
Then I'll turn your own people against you.
Then we ride to the Keepers of the Pact.
